# JS Sushi — Complete Documentation

> An interactive browser game that teaches JavaScript array methods through a sushi conveyor belt metaphor.

JS Sushi is a free, browser-based coding game where students write real JavaScript to manipulate a visual sushi conveyor belt. It covers 40 levels across 7 chapters, progressing from array basics to advanced patterns like map, filter, reduce, spread, and destructuring.

## URL

https://denn1s.github.io/js-sushi/

## How It Works

Each level presents a coding challenge in a single-line editor. Students write one line of JavaScript that manipulates an array (represented as a sushi belt). When the code runs, the belt animates to show the result. Correct solutions advance the student to the next level.

- No build step, no setup — runs entirely in the browser
- Uses CodeMirror 6 for the code editor
- Sandboxed code execution via `new Function()`
- Progress saved to localStorage
- No semicolons allowed in student code
- Single-line editor (Enter key is blocked)

## Architecture

- **No build step** — ES modules loaded directly via `<script type="module">` and importmap for CodeMirror
- **CodeMirror 6** loaded from esm.sh CDN
- **Single belt display** — one `#belt-display` container shows initial state on load, transitions to result on success
- **Locked prefix** — levels can define non-editable dimmed text at start of editor
- **Catppuccin Mocha** dark color theme throughout

### Project Structure

```
index.html          — Single-page app entry point
js/
  main.js           — App init, level navigation, code execution flow
  levels/           — Level definitions split by chapter
    index.js        — Re-exports all chapters as a single levels array
    chapter1.js     — The Empty Belt (levels 1-6)
    chapter2.js     — Adding & Removing (levels 7-15)
    chapter3.js     — Searching the Belt (levels 16-21)
    chapter4.js     — Transforming the Belt (levels 22-28)
    chapter5.js     — Sorting & Rearranging (level 29)
    chapter6.js     — Spread & Copying (levels 30-34)
    chapter7.js     — Destructuring (levels 35-40)
  engine.js         — Sandboxed code execution, input sanitization
  editor.js         — CodeMirror 6 editor with locked prefix support
  belt.js           — Belt SVG visualization controller
  ui.js             — UI rendering, level selector, feedback messages
  progress.js       — localStorage persistence
css/
  style.css         — Main layout
  belt.css          — Belt animations
  variables.css     — Catppuccin Mocha color palette
svg/
  mascot.svg        — Sushi mascot character
  sushi/            — Individual sushi piece SVGs (salmon, tuna, shrimp, eel, squid)
  plates/           — Colored plate SVGs for level badges
```

## Chapters and Levels

### Chapter 1: The Empty Belt (Levels 1-6)
Array creation, access by index, mutation.
- Level 1: Create an array with 'salmon'
- Level 2: Access array elements by index
- Level 3: Get array length
- Level 4: Modify an element
- Level 5: Access the last element
- Level 6: Create a multi-item array

### Chapter 2: Adding & Removing (Levels 7-15)
push, pop, unshift, shift, splice, slice.
- Levels 7-8: push (add to end)
- Level 9: pop (remove from end)
- Level 10: unshift (add to front)
- Level 11: shift (remove from front)
- Levels 12-13: splice (insert/remove at position)
- Levels 14-15: slice (extract portion)

### Chapter 3: Searching the Belt (Levels 16-21)
includes, indexOf, find, findIndex, every.
- Level 16: includes (check if item exists)
- Level 17: indexOf (find position)
- Level 18-19: find (locate by condition)
- Level 20: findIndex (locate index by condition)
- Level 21: every (test all elements)

### Chapter 4: Transforming the Belt (Levels 22-28)
filter, map, reduce, working with objects.
- Levels 22-23: filter (keep matching items)
- Levels 24-25: map (transform each item)
- Levels 26-27: reduce (combine into single value)
- Level 28: Working with arrays of objects

### Chapter 5: Sorting & Rearranging (Level 29)
sort with custom comparison functions.
- Level 29: Custom sort with comparator

### Chapter 6: Spread & Copying (Levels 30-34)
Copy trap, spread copy, safe copy, spread add.
- Level 30: The copy trap (reference vs value)
- Level 31: Spread copy
- Level 32: Safe copy
- Levels 33-34: Spread to add/combine

### Chapter 7: Destructuring (Levels 35-40)
Array destructuring, object destructuring, rest syntax, final challenge.
- Level 35: Pick first element
- Level 36: Rest syntax
- Level 37: Skip elements
- Level 38-39: Object destructuring
- Level 40: Final challenge combining concepts

## Topics Covered

- Array literal syntax: `['salmon', 'tuna']`
- Accessing elements: `belt[0]`, `belt.length`
- Modifying arrays: `push()`, `pop()`, `unshift()`, `shift()`
- Slicing and splicing: `slice()`, `splice()`
- Searching: `includes()`, `indexOf()`, `find()`, `findIndex()`, `every()`
- Transforming: `filter()`, `map()`, `reduce()`
- Sorting: `sort()` with comparators
- Spread operator: `[...belt]`, `[...a, ...b]`
- Destructuring: `const [first, ...rest] = belt`

## Sushi Items

Five sushi types with distinct colors:
- **Salmon** (#fa8072) — coral/orange
- **Tuna** (#dc3545) — red
- **Shrimp** (#f8a4b8) — pink
- **Eel** (#8b6914) — brown/gold
- **Squid** (#dda0dd) — plum/purple

## Tech Stack

- Vanilla JavaScript (ES modules, no framework, no build step)
- CodeMirror 6 (loaded from esm.sh CDN)
- Catppuccin Mocha dark color theme
- Google Fonts: IBM Plex Mono
- Single HTML page

## Target Audience

Beginner to intermediate JavaScript learners who want hands-on practice with array methods in an engaging, visual format. Ideal for:
- Computer science students learning JavaScript
- Self-taught developers wanting array method practice
- Teachers looking for interactive JavaScript exercises
- Anyone who learns better through visual, hands-on coding
